summary: I worked for several years as a field person with the NAACP, traveling throughout the South during the 1930s, 40s, and early 50s. At that time, the NAACP was the main organization of any real importance in the struggle for black rights. We focused on legal challenges against discriminatory practices, such as equalizing teacher salaries and fighting segregation in state universities like the University of Maryland. Leaders like Harry T. Moore and lawyers like Charlie Houston were instrumental in these efforts.

Harry T. Moore was a key figure in Florida's civil rights movement and a leader within the NAACP. I knew him well, and I remember the tragic night in 1946 when his house in Mims, Florida was bombed on Christmas, killing him and his wife. His death was a stark reminder of the violent resistance to civil rights, and despite the FBI under Herbert Hoover's leadership, no clues were ever found. Moore was also one of the black educators fired for pushing equal pay for black teachers.

Mims, Florida is where Harry T. Moore and his wife were killed in a bombing on Christmas night in 1946. This small place became a tragic symbol of the violent backlash against civil rights activism in the South. I recall this event vividly as it highlighted the dangers faced by activists like Moore who were fighting for equality in the region.

Herbert Hoover, the FBI director at the time of Harry T. Moore's murder, was often praised, but I think it's important to note that under his watch, the FBI never found any clues about the bombing in Mims, Florida. This failure reflects the limits of federal law enforcement in protecting civil rights activists during that era. Hoover's legacy in this context is quite controversial.

The Southern Conference Educational Fund, which the Bradens were part of, was active in the South before 1960. Its initial meeting was held in Birmingham, Alabama, where Bull Connor was already arresting people for mixed gatherings. This organization played a role in the early civil rights struggles, supporting integrated efforts and educational initiatives in the region.

Birmingham was a focal point of civil rights struggles long before the 1960s. It had a repressive atmosphere, but professionals and labor leaders, including those from the United Mine Workers and Steel Workers, fought against segregation and discrimination there. Bull Connor was a notorious figure in Birmingham, known for arresting people involved in civil rights activities, including those at early meetings of groups like the Southern Conference Educational Fund.

Bull Connor was infamous in Birmingham for his harsh tactics against civil rights activists. Even before the well-known events of the late 1950s and 60s, he was arresting people for mixed gatherings, such as at the Southern Conference Educational Fund's initial meeting. His role as a symbol of segregationist resistance was well established by the time the sit-in movement began.

In Birmingham, the United Mine Workers were part of the labor movement that intersected with civil rights struggles. Some individuals involved in these unions also took stands against segregation, contributing to the broader fight for equality. Their involvement showed that the movement included people from various social and professional backgrounds.

John LaFlore was a pioneer in challenging segregation, especially in Alabama. He worked for the Post Office in Mobile and was known for insisting on riding first-class on trains, traveling from Mobile to Atlanta to test segregation laws. His activism led to persecution, especially in the 1950s, and he almost lost his job because of his stands against discrimination.

Mobile was the home of John LaFlore, a civil rights pioneer who challenged segregation in public transportation. It was a place where activists faced persecution for their efforts, as LaFlore nearly lost his postal job due to his activism. Mobile's role in the movement is often overlooked but was significant in the broader Southern struggle for equality.

I was in Atlanta in the early 1960s working to set up the office of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference. Atlanta served as a hub for coordinating civil rights activities across the South. It was also the site where student leaders from various cities met to establish communication and coordination during the sit-in movement. The city was central to the leadership and organizational efforts of the movement.

In Norfolk, Virginia, young people were involved in early civil rights efforts such as challenging unequal teacher salaries. I recall a young woman there who was one of the first to test this issue. Norfolk was one of the many places where the NAACP worked to equalize educational opportunities before the student activism of the 1960s took hold.

William Hasty was an important figure in the NAACP's legal efforts, particularly on education and equalization. He worked alongside his cousin Charlie Houston in developing programs to challenge segregation and discrimination. Hasty eventually became a federal judge in Philadelphia, showing the long-term impact of his civil rights work.

Philadelphia was where William Hasty, a key NAACP legal strategist, ended up serving as a federal judge. Though much of the early civil rights work I was involved in took place in the South, Philadelphia represents the broader reach and eventual institutional influence of activists like Hasty. It shows how the movement's leaders moved into positions of judicial authority over time.

Charlie Houston was one of the architects of the NAACP's legal strategies to fight segregation, particularly in education. He worked closely with William Hasty on programs to equalize educational facilities and teacher salaries. Houston's work laid the groundwork for many of the legal battles that challenged discriminatory practices in the South.

The University of Maryland was one of the first state universities challenged by the NAACP for its discriminatory practices. Our legal efforts targeted segregation in higher education, and Maryland was a key battleground in these early fights. This was part of a broader strategy to dismantle segregation through the courts before the mass student activism of the 1960s.

The Montgomery bus boycott was a historic event where about 50,000 black people in Montgomery, Alabama, chose to walk rather than ride segregated buses. This happened before the sit-in movement and set a powerful example of mass nonviolent protest. It was a key moment that helped inspire and give momentum to the student sit-ins and other civil rights actions that followed.

Montgomery was the site of the famous bus boycott, where thousands of black residents protested segregation by refusing to ride city buses. This event was a turning point in the civil rights movement and influenced later actions like the sit-ins. The boycott demonstrated the power of organized, nonviolent mass protest in the South.

I was the Executive Secretary of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference in the early 1960s, based in Atlanta. The SCLC was founded to be a Southern-based mass membership organization advocating nonviolent protest. When the sit-in movement began, I helped organize meetings of student leaders to foster communication and coordination, framing it as a leadership training effort under the SCLC's philosophy.

Greensboro was the birthplace of the sit-in movement in 1960, where students like Blair started protesting segregation at lunch counters. I tried to arrange a meeting of student leaders there, but due to scheduling conflicts, we held it in Raleigh instead. Greensboro's sit-ins sparked a wave of activism across the South and beyond.

Raleigh became the site of a crucial meeting of student leaders during the early sit-in movement, held at Shaw University. I secured space there and helped organize the gathering to promote communication and coordination among activists from various cities. This meeting was important in shaping what would become the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee.

Durham was one of the cities where student activism was emerging during the sit-in movement. It was part of the network of Southern cities involved in early civil rights protests, and students from Durham participated in meetings to coordinate efforts. The city's involvement helped spread the movement across the region.

Shaw University in Raleigh hosted the important early meeting of student leaders from various Southern cities during the sit-in movement. The university provided space for activists to come together and discuss communication and coordination strategies. This meeting was a foundational moment leading to the formation of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee.

The Congress of Racial Equality, or CORE, was one of the established organizations involved in the civil rights movement at the time the student sit-ins began. Some forces within CORE sought to attach the young activists to existing groups, but the students preferred to maintain their own identity. CORE's presence was part of the broader landscape of civil rights organizations interacting with the emerging student movement.

The Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, or SNCC, emerged from the early student meetings I helped organize in 1960. Initially called the Temporary Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee at a meeting in Raleigh, it was formed because students wanted their own organization rather than joining existing groups like the SCLC or CORE. SNCC became a vital force in coordinating sit-ins and other nonviolent protests across the South.
